entries.d: Descent of the Holy Spirit upon the Apostles in Jerusalem — the birthday of the Church (Acts 2).

sections.b: Descent of the Holy Spirit upon the Apostles in Jerusalem — the birthday of the Church (Acts 2).

entries.d: The Apostles rule that Gentile converts are not bound by the Mosaic law (Acts 15) — the pattern for all later councils.

sections.b: The Apostles rule that Gentile converts are not bound by the Mosaic law (Acts 15) — the pattern for all later councils.

entries.d: Jerusalem falls to the Romans under Titus; the Temple is destroyed, scattering Jewish Christianity.

sections.b: Jerusalem falls to the Romans under Titus; the Temple is destroyed, scattering Jewish Christianity.

entries.d: Constantine and Licinius grant freedom of worship, ending three centuries of intermittent persecution.

sections.b: Constantine and Licinius grant freedom of worship, ending three centuries of intermittent persecution.

entries.d: Theodosius makes Nicene Christianity the official religion of the Roman Empire.

sections.b: Theodosius makes Nicene Christianity the official religion of the Roman Empire.

entries.d: St. Jerome finishes his Latin translation of the Scriptures at Bethlehem — the Bible of the West for a millennium.

sections.b: St. Jerome finishes his Latin translation of the Scriptures at Bethlehem — the Bible of the West for a millennium.

entries.d: Rome's last western emperor is deposed; the Church becomes the great preserver of classical learning.

sections.b: Rome's last western emperor is deposed; the Church becomes the great preserver of classical learning.

entries.d: The king of the Franks is baptized at Reims; France becomes the 'eldest daughter of the Church.'

sections.b: The king of the Franks is baptized at Reims; France becomes the 'eldest daughter of the Church.'

entries.d: The Muslim conquest sweeps through the ancient Christian heartlands of the Levant and, soon, North Africa.

sections.b: The Muslim conquest sweeps through the ancient Christian heartlands of the Levant and, soon, North Africa.

entries.d: Charles Martel halts the Muslim advance into Western Europe.

sections.b: Charles Martel halts the Muslim advance into Western Europe.

entries.d: Pope Leo III crowns Charlemagne emperor on Christmas Day — the birth of medieval Christendom.

sections.b: Pope Leo III crowns Charlemagne emperor on Christmas Day — the birth of medieval Christendom.

entries.d: The abbey of Cluny launches the great monastic reform movement of the Middle Ages.

sections.b: The abbey of Cluny launches the great monastic reform movement of the Middle Ages.

entries.d: Prince Vladimir of Kyiv is baptized, and the Christianization of the Rus begins.

sections.b: Prince Vladimir of Kyiv is baptized, and the Christianization of the Rus begins.

entries.d: Mutual excommunications in Constantinople mark the tragic break between the Catholic West and Orthodox East.

sections.b: Mutual excommunications in Constantinople mark the tragic break between the Catholic West and Orthodox East.

entries.d: Urban II calls the First Crusade at Clermont; Jerusalem is taken in 1099.

sections.b: Urban II calls the First Crusade at Clermont; Jerusalem is taken in 1099.

entries.d: Cathedral schools grow into the universities of Bologna, Paris, and Oxford — cradle of scholastic theology.

sections.b: Cathedral schools grow into the universities of Bologna, Paris, and Oxford — cradle of scholastic theology.

entries.d: The popes reside in Avignon, France for nearly seventy years — the 'Babylonian Captivity' of the papacy.

sections.b: The popes reside in Avignon, France for nearly seventy years — the 'Babylonian Captivity' of the papacy.

entries.d: Two, and later three, rival claimants to the papacy divide Europe until the Council of Constance.

sections.b: Two, and later three, rival claimants to the papacy divide Europe until the Council of Constance.

entries.d: The Byzantine Empire ends as the Ottomans take Constantinople; Greek scholars flee west.

sections.b: The Byzantine Empire ends as the Ottomans take Constantinople; Greek scholars flee west.

entries.d: The first major printed book is the Latin Vulgate — a media revolution for Scripture and doctrine alike.

sections.b: The first major printed book is the Latin Vulgate — a media revolution for Scripture and doctrine alike.

entries.d: Catholic evangelization of the Americas begins in the wake of Columbus's voyages.

sections.b: Catholic evangelization of the Americas begins in the wake of Columbus's voyages.

entries.d: Martin Luther's protest at Wittenberg ignites the Protestant Reformation.

sections.b: Martin Luther's protest at Wittenberg ignites the Protestant Reformation.

entries.d: Henry VIII breaks with Rome and declares himself head of the Church in England.

sections.b: Henry VIII breaks with Rome and declares himself head of the Church in England.

entries.d: The Holy League's naval victory, credited by St. Pius V to the Rosary, gives us the feast of Our Lady of the Rosary.

sections.b: The Holy League's naval victory, credited by St. Pius V to the Rosary, gives us the feast of Our Lady of the Rosary.

entries.d: Gregory XIII reforms the calendar still used by most of the world today.

sections.b: Gregory XIII reforms the calendar still used by most of the world today.

entries.d: Rome establishes its great missionary congregation to coordinate evangelization worldwide.

sections.b: Rome establishes its great missionary congregation to coordinate evangelization worldwide.

entries.d: Clement XIV suppresses the Society of Jesus under political pressure; it is restored in 1814.

sections.b: Clement XIV suppresses the Society of Jesus under political pressure; it is restored in 1814.

entries.d: Bl. Pius IX solemnly defines that Mary was conceived without original sin (Ineffabilis Deus).

sections.b: Bl. Pius IX solemnly defines that Mary was conceived without original sin (Ineffabilis Deus).

entries.d: Italian forces take Rome; the pope becomes the 'prisoner of the Vatican' until 1929.

sections.b: Italian forces take Rome; the pope becomes the 'prisoner of the Vatican' until 1929.

entries.d: Leo XIII's encyclical on capital and labor launches modern Catholic social teaching.

sections.b: Leo XIII's encyclical on capital and labor launches modern Catholic social teaching.

entries.d: St. Josemaría Escrivá founds Opus Dei in Madrid, preaching sanctification through ordinary work and daily life.

sections.b: St. Josemaría Escrivá founds Opus Dei in Madrid, preaching sanctification through ordinary work and daily life.

entries.d: The Vatican City State is established, resolving the 'Roman Question.'

sections.b: The Vatican City State is established, resolving the 'Roman Question.'

entries.d: Pius XII solemnly defines the Assumption of Mary, body and soul, into heaven (Munificentissimus Deus).

sections.b: Pius XII solemnly defines the Assumption of Mary, body and soul, into heaven (Munificentissimus Deus).

entries.d: The first Polish pope begins a 27-year pontificate that helps bring down communism.

sections.b: The first Polish pope begins a 27-year pontificate that helps bring down communism.

entries.d: St. John Paul II promulgates the Catechism of the Catholic Church, the first universal catechism since Trent.

sections.b: St. John Paul II promulgates the Catechism of the Catholic Church, the first universal catechism since Trent.

entries.d: Benedict XVI becomes the first pope to resign in six centuries; Francis is the first pope from the Americas.

sections.b: Benedict XVI becomes the first pope to resign in six centuries; Francis is the first pope from the Americas.

entries.d: After the death of Pope Francis, Leo XIV is elected — the first American-born pope.

sections.b: After the death of Pope Francis, Leo XIV is elected — the first American-born pope.

entries.d: By ancient tradition, Mary appeared to St. James the Greater at Zaragoza while still living in Jerusalem — the first Marian apparition.

sections.b: By ancient tradition, Mary appeared to St. James the Greater at Zaragoza while still living in Jerusalem — the first Marian apparition.

entries.d: Mary asks Richeldis de Faverches to build a replica of the Holy House of Nazareth — England's great medieval shrine.

sections.b: Mary asks Richeldis de Faverches to build a replica of the Holy House of Nazareth — England's great medieval shrine.

entries.d: Mary appears to St. Juan Diego at Tepeyac; millions of Aztecs enter the Church within a decade.

sections.b: Mary appears to St. Juan Diego at Tepeyac; millions of Aztecs enter the Church within a decade.

entries.d: Mary appears to St. Catherine Labouré in Paris and asks for the medal to be struck: 'O Mary, conceived without sin…'

sections.b: Mary appears to St. Catherine Labouré in Paris and asks for the medal to be struck: 'O Mary, conceived without sin…'

entries.d: Mary appears weeping to two shepherd children in the French Alps, calling for conversion and penance.

sections.b: Mary appears weeping to two shepherd children in the French Alps, calling for conversion and penance.

entries.d: Mary appears to St. Bernadette: 'I am the Immaculate Conception.' Lourdes becomes a place of healing.

sections.b: Mary appears to St. Bernadette: 'I am the Immaculate Conception.' Lourdes becomes a place of healing.

entries.d: Mary appears to Adele Brise near Champion, Wisconsin — the first approved Marian apparition in the United States.

sections.b: Mary appears to Adele Brise near Champion, Wisconsin — the first approved Marian apparition in the United States.

entries.d: During the Franco-Prussian War Mary appears: 'But pray, my children. God will hear you in a little while.'

sections.b: During the Franco-Prussian War Mary appears: 'But pray, my children. God will hear you in a little while.'

entries.d: Mary, St. Joseph, and St. John the Evangelist appear silently at the parish gable in County Mayo, Ireland.

sections.b: Mary, St. Joseph, and St. John the Evangelist appear silently at the parish gable in County Mayo, Ireland.

entries.d: Mary appears to three shepherd children in Portugal; the Miracle of the Sun is witnessed by tens of thousands.

sections.b: Mary appears to three shepherd children in Portugal; the Miracle of the Sun is witnessed by tens of thousands.

entries.d: Mary appears in Belgium as the 'Virgin of the Golden Heart' (1932) and the 'Virgin of the Poor' (1933).

sections.b: Mary appears in Belgium as the 'Virgin of the Golden Heart' (1932) and the 'Virgin of the Poor' (1933).

entries.d: Weeping statue and messages to Sr. Agnes Sasagawa in Japan; approved by the local bishop in 1984.

sections.b: Weeping statue and messages to Sr. Agnes Sasagawa in Japan; approved by the local bishop in 1984.

entries.d: A doubting monk's host becomes flesh and the wine blood; the flesh, later identified as cardiac tissue, is still preserved.

sections.b: A doubting monk's host becomes flesh and the wine blood; the flesh, later identified as cardiac tissue, is still preserved.

entries.d: A stolen host bleeds; enshrined in Portugal and venerated for nearly eight centuries.

sections.b: A stolen host bleeds; enshrined in Portugal and venerated for nearly eight centuries.

entries.d: A host bleeds onto the corporal at Mass; Urban IV institutes the feast of Corpus Christi the following year.

sections.b: A host bleeds onto the corporal at Mass; Urban IV institutes the feast of Corpus Christi the following year.

entries.d: A host survives fire unscathed — origin of the yearly 'Stille Omgang' silent procession.

sections.b: A host survives fire unscathed — origin of the yearly 'Stille Omgang' silent procession.

entries.d: A discarded host becomes bleeding tissue; studies were commissioned under Archbishop Bergoglio, the future Pope Francis.

sections.b: A discarded host becomes bleeding tissue; studies were commissioned under Archbishop Bergoglio, the future Pope Francis.

entries.d: A dropped host develops tissue identified as heart muscle; approved for veneration in Poland.

sections.b: A dropped host develops tissue identified as heart muscle; approved for veneration in Poland.

entries.d: First ecumenical council: condemns Arianism and confesses the Son as consubstantial (homoousios) with the Father.

sections.b: First ecumenical council: condemns Arianism and confesses the Son as consubstantial (homoousios) with the Father.

entries.d: Affirms the divinity of the Holy Spirit; gives the Church the Nicene-Constantinopolitan Creed.

sections.b: Affirms the divinity of the Holy Spirit; gives the Church the Nicene-Constantinopolitan Creed.

entries.d: Declares Mary truly Theotokos — Mother of God — and condemns Nestorius.

sections.b: Declares Mary truly Theotokos — Mother of God — and condemns Nestorius.

entries.d: Defines Christ as one Person in two natures, divine and human, guided by the Tome of St. Leo.

sections.b: Defines Christ as one Person in two natures, divine and human, guided by the Tome of St. Leo.

entries.d: Condemns the 'Three Chapters' and reaffirms Chalcedonian Christology.

sections.b: Condemns the 'Three Chapters' and reaffirms Chalcedonian Christology.

entries.d: Affirms two wills in Christ, divine and human, against Monothelitism.

sections.b: Affirms two wills in Christ, divine and human, against Monothelitism.

entries.d: Vindicates the veneration of holy icons against the iconoclasts.

sections.b: Vindicates the veneration of holy icons against the iconoclasts.

entries.d: Resolves the Photian controversy — the last council held in the East.

sections.b: Resolves the Photian controversy — the last council held in the East.

entries.d: First ecumenical council in the West, confirming the settlement of the investiture controversy.

sections.b: First ecumenical council in the West, confirming the settlement of the investiture controversy.

entries.d: Reform council strengthening the discipline of clerical celibacy.

sections.b: Reform council strengthening the discipline of clerical celibacy.

entries.d: Establishes the two-thirds majority for papal elections still in force today.

sections.b: Establishes the two-thirds majority for papal elections still in force today.

entries.d: The great medieval council: 'transubstantiation' enters conciliar language; annual confession and Communion required.

sections.b: The great medieval council: 'transubstantiation' enters conciliar language; annual confession and Communion required.

entries.d: Deposes Emperor Frederick II amid the struggle of papacy and empire.

sections.b: Deposes Emperor Frederick II amid the struggle of papacy and empire.

entries.d: Achieves a brief reunion with the Greek Church; St. Thomas Aquinas dies en route.

sections.b: Achieves a brief reunion with the Greek Church; St. Thomas Aquinas dies en route.

entries.d: Suppresses the Knights Templar and addresses reform.

sections.b: Suppresses the Knights Templar and addresses reform.

entries.d: Ends the Western Schism by electing Martin V as sole pope.

sections.b: Ends the Western Schism by electing Martin V as sole pope.

entries.d: Decrees of union with the Greek and other Eastern churches (1439), though short-lived.

sections.b: Decrees of union with the Greek and other Eastern churches (1439), though short-lived.

entries.d: Reform decrees on the very eve of the Reformation.

sections.b: Reform decrees on the very eve of the Reformation.

entries.d: The great answer to the Reformation: justification, Scripture and Tradition, the sacraments, and the Mass, over 18 years.

sections.b: The great answer to the Reformation: justification, Scripture and Tradition, the sacraments, and the Mass, over 18 years.

entries.d: Defines papal infallibility and the primacy of the Roman Pontiff (Pastor Aeternus).

sections.b: Defines papal infallibility and the primacy of the Roman Pontiff (Pastor Aeternus).

entries.d: Renewal of liturgy, ecclesiology, revelation, and the Church in the modern world: Lumen Gentium, Dei Verbum, and more.

sections.b: Renewal of liturgy, ecclesiology, revelation, and the Church in the modern world: Lumen Gentium, Dei Verbum, and more.

entries.d: After the Great Fire of Rome, Nero blames the Christians — the first imperial persecution; Peter and Paul are martyred.

sections.b: After the Great Fire of Rome, Nero blames the Christians — the first imperial persecution; Peter and Paul are martyred.

entries.d: Sporadic persecution; tradition places St. John's exile to Patmos in these years.

sections.b: Sporadic persecution; tradition places St. John's exile to Patmos in these years.

entries.d: Pliny's letter and Trajan's reply set the pattern: Christians punished if denounced and unrepentant.

sections.b: Pliny's letter and Trajan's reply set the pattern: Christians punished if denounced and unrepentant.

entries.d: Under Marcus Aurelius, a pogrom in Gaul claims Blandina, Pothinus, and companions.

sections.b: Under Marcus Aurelius, a pogrom in Gaul claims Blandina, Pothinus, and companions.

entries.d: The first empire-wide edict requires sacrifice to the gods; the crisis of the lapsi follows.

sections.b: The first empire-wide edict requires sacrifice to the gods; the crisis of the lapsi follows.

entries.d: Clergy are targeted; Sts. Cyprian, Sixtus II, and Lawrence are among the martyrs.

sections.b: Clergy are targeted; Sts. Cyprian, Sixtus II, and Lawrence are among the martyrs.

entries.d: Diocletian unleashes the fiercest and final empire-wide persecution — churches razed, Scriptures burned.

sections.b: Diocletian unleashes the fiercest and final empire-wide persecution — churches razed, Scriptures burned.

entries.d: Under Shapur II, tens of thousands of Christians in the Persian Empire are martyred over four decades.

sections.b: Under Shapur II, tens of thousands of Christians in the Persian Empire are martyred over four decades.

entries.d: From Sts. More and Fisher to the Forty Martyrs of England and Wales, Catholics die under the Tudor and Stuart penal laws.

sections.b: From Sts. More and Fisher to the Forty Martyrs of England and Wales, Catholics die under the Tudor and Stuart penal laws.

entries.d: Christianity is banned; thousands are martyred and the 'hidden Christians' endure for 250 years.

sections.b: Christianity is banned; thousands are martyred and the 'hidden Christians' endure for 250 years.

entries.d: Dechristianization: priests and religious are guillotined or drowned; the Carmelites of Compiègne die singing (1794).

sections.b: Dechristianization: priests and religious are guillotined or drowned; the Carmelites of Compiègne die singing (1794).

entries.d: Up to 130,000 Christians are killed across several waves; 117 martyrs are canonized in 1988.

sections.b: Up to 130,000 Christians are killed across several waves; 117 martyrs are canonized in 1988.

entries.d: Thousands of Korean Catholics are martyred (1839, 1846, 1866), including St. Andrew Kim Taegon.

sections.b: Thousands of Korean Catholics are martyred (1839, 1846, 1866), including St. Andrew Kim Taegon.

entries.d: Armenian, Assyrian, and Greek Christians perish en masse; Bl. Ignatius Maloyan is among the martyrs.

sections.b: Armenian, Assyrian, and Greek Christians perish en masse; Bl. Ignatius Maloyan is among the martyrs.

entries.d: Mexico's Calles Laws suppress the Church; Bl. Miguel Pro dies crying '¡Viva Cristo Rey!' (1927).

sections.b: Mexico's Calles Laws suppress the Church; Bl. Miguel Pro dies crying '¡Viva Cristo Rey!' (1927).

entries.d: Thousands of priests, religious, and laity are killed in the anticlerical terror; many are now beatified.

sections.b: Thousands of priests, religious, and laity are killed in the anticlerical terror; many are now beatified.

entries.d: The Church is suppressed across the Eastern bloc and China; Cardinal Mindszenty's show trial becomes a symbol.

sections.b: The Church is suppressed across the Eastern bloc and China; Cardinal Mindszenty's show trial becomes a symbol.

entries.d: Prince of the Apostles and first pope, martyred in Rome under Nero — by tradition crucified upside down.

sections.b: Prince of the Apostles and first pope, martyred in Rome under Nero — by tradition crucified upside down.

entries.d: Apostle to the Gentiles, author of the epistles, beheaded in Rome under Nero.

sections.b: Apostle to the Gentiles, author of the epistles, beheaded in Rome under Nero.

entries.d: The beloved disciple and evangelist; the only apostle not martyred, dying at Ephesus.

sections.b: The beloved disciple and evangelist; the only apostle not martyred, dying at Ephesus.

entries.d: Bishop of Antioch, fed to beasts in Rome; his seven letters witness to the episcopacy and the Eucharist.

sections.b: Bishop of Antioch, fed to beasts in Rome; his seven letters witness to the episcopacy and the Eucharist.

entries.d: Disciple of John and bishop of Smyrna, burned at the stake at age 86: 'Eighty-six years I have served Him.'

sections.b: Disciple of John and bishop of Smyrna, burned at the stake at age 86: 'Eighty-six years I have served Him.'

entries.d: Philosopher-apologist beheaded in Rome; his First Apology describes the early Mass.

sections.b: Philosopher-apologist beheaded in Rome; his First Apology describes the early Mass.

entries.d: Bishop of Lyons; his Against Heresies defends apostolic tradition and succession.

sections.b: Bishop of Lyons; his Against Heresies defends apostolic tradition and succession.

entries.d: Young mother and expectant slave martyred together in the Carthage arena; Perpetua's prison diary survives.

sections.b: Young mother and expectant slave martyred together in the Carthage arena; Perpetua's prison diary survives.

entries.d: Roman virgin martyr, patroness of music and musicians.

sections.b: Roman virgin martyr, patroness of music and musicians.

entries.d: Deacon of Rome, roasted on a gridiron after presenting the poor as 'the treasures of the Church.'

sections.b: Deacon of Rome, roasted on a gridiron after presenting the poor as 'the treasures of the Church.'

entries.d: Bishop of Carthage and martyr under Valerian; wrote On the Unity of the Catholic Church.

sections.b: Bishop of Carthage and martyr under Valerian; wrote On the Unity of the Catholic Church.

entries.d: Virgin martyr of about twelve years under Diocletian; her name is in the Roman Canon.

sections.b: Virgin martyr of about twelve years under Diocletian; her name is in the Roman Canon.

entries.d: Father of monasticism; his life by Athanasius set the desert ablaze with monks.

sections.b: Father of monasticism; his life by Athanasius set the desert ablaze with monks.

entries.d: Bishop of Alexandria, exiled five times for defending the Nicene faith against Arianism.

sections.b: Bishop of Alexandria, exiled five times for defending the Nicene faith against Arianism.

entries.d: Cappadocian Father, monastic legislator, and builder of the first great complex for the sick and poor.

sections.b: Cappadocian Father, monastic legislator, and builder of the first great complex for the sick and poor.

entries.d: Bishop whose Catechetical Lectures remain a treasury of sacramental teaching.

sections.b: Bishop whose Catechetical Lectures remain a treasury of sacramental teaching.

entries.d: Mother of Augustine, whose years of tears and prayer won her son's conversion.

sections.b: Mother of Augustine, whose years of tears and prayer won her son's conversion.

entries.d: 'The Theologian' — defender of the Trinity and the divinity of the Holy Spirit.

sections.b: 'The Theologian' — defender of the Trinity and the divinity of the Holy Spirit.

entries.d: Bishop of Milan who baptized Augustine and faced down emperors at the church door.

sections.b: Bishop of Milan who baptized Augustine and faced down emperors at the church door.

entries.d: The 'golden-mouthed' preacher of Constantinople, who died in exile for rebuking the powerful.

sections.b: The 'golden-mouthed' preacher of Constantinople, who died in exile for rebuking the powerful.

entries.d: Irascible genius of Bethlehem who gave the West its Latin Bible: 'Ignorance of Scripture is ignorance of Christ.'

sections.b: Irascible genius of Bethlehem who gave the West its Latin Bible: 'Ignorance of Scripture is ignorance of Christ.'

entries.d: Bishop of Hippo, author of the Confessions and City of God — the greatest of the Latin Fathers.

sections.b: Bishop of Hippo, author of the Confessions and City of God — the greatest of the Latin Fathers.

entries.d: Champion of the title Theotokos at the Council of Ephesus.

sections.b: Champion of the title Theotokos at the Council of Ephesus.

entries.d: Former slave who returned as apostle of Ireland; his Confession still speaks.

sections.b: Former slave who returned as apostle of Ireland; his Confession still speaks.

entries.d: Pope whose Tome shaped Chalcedon and who turned Attila the Hun back from Rome.

sections.b: Pope whose Tome shaped Chalcedon and who turned Attila the Hun back from Rome.

entries.d: Abbess and co-patroness of Ireland, famed for boundless charity.

sections.b: Abbess and co-patroness of Ireland, famed for boundless charity.

entries.d: Father of Western monasticism; his Rule ('ora et labora') built Europe from Monte Cassino outward.

sections.b: Father of Western monasticism; his Rule ('ora et labora') built Europe from Monte Cassino outward.

entries.d: Irish monk of Iona, apostle to the Picts of Scotland.

sections.b: Irish monk of Iona, apostle to the Picts of Scotland.

entries.d: Pope and Doctor who sent missionaries to England and gave his name to the Church's chant.

sections.b: Pope and Doctor who sent missionaries to England and gave his name to the Church's chant.

entries.d: Sent by Gregory, he became the first archbishop of Canterbury and apostle of the English.

sections.b: Sent by Gregory, he became the first archbishop of Canterbury and apostle of the English.

entries.d: Encyclopedist of the ancient world's learning — often called the last Father of the West.

sections.b: Encyclopedist of the ancient world's learning — often called the last Father of the West.

entries.d: Theologian who lost tongue and hand rather than deny the two wills of Christ.

sections.b: Theologian who lost tongue and hand rather than deny the two wills of Christ.

entries.d: Monk of Jarrow, Doctor of the Church, and father of English history.

sections.b: Monk of Jarrow, Doctor of the Church, and father of English history.

entries.d: Monk of Mar Saba who defended the holy icons — the last of the Greek Fathers.

sections.b: Monk of Mar Saba who defended the holy icons — the last of the Greek Fathers.

entries.d: Apostle of Germany who felled Donar's sacred oak; martyred in Frisia with the Gospel book in his hands.

sections.b: Apostle of Germany who felled Donar's sacred oak; martyred in Frisia with the Gospel book in his hands.

entries.d: Apostles of the Slavs, who created an alphabet and liturgy in the Slavonic tongue.

sections.b: Apostles of the Slavs, who created an alphabet and liturgy in the Slavonic tongue.

entries.d: Duke of Bohemia, murdered by his brother at the church door — the 'Good King' of the carol.

sections.b: Duke of Bohemia, murdered by his brother at the church door — the 'Good King' of the carol.

entries.d: First king of Hungary, who led the Magyars into the Church.

sections.b: First king of Hungary, who led the Magyars into the Church.

entries.d: King of England and builder of Westminster Abbey.

sections.b: King of England and builder of Westminster Abbey.

entries.d: Archbishop of Canterbury: 'faith seeking understanding' and the ontological argument.

sections.b: Archbishop of Canterbury: 'faith seeking understanding' and the ontological argument.

entries.d: Cistercian reformer and 'last of the Fathers'; the Mellifluous Doctor of Marian devotion.

sections.b: Cistercian reformer and 'last of the Fathers'; the Mellifluous Doctor of Marian devotion.

entries.d: Archbishop murdered in Canterbury Cathedral by Henry II's knights, defending the Church's liberty.

sections.b: Archbishop murdered in Canterbury Cathedral by Henry II's knights, defending the Church's liberty.

entries.d: Benedictine abbess, visionary, composer, and Doctor of the Church.

sections.b: Benedictine abbess, visionary, composer, and Doctor of the Church.

entries.d: Spanish founder of the Order of Preachers, dedicated to truth and the salvation of souls; died at Bologna.

sections.b: Spanish founder of the Order of Preachers, dedicated to truth and the salvation of souls; died at Bologna.

entries.d: The Poverello, bearing the stigmata; rebuilder of the Church and lover of Lady Poverty.

sections.b: The Poverello, bearing the stigmata; rebuilder of the Church and lover of Lady Poverty.

entries.d: Portuguese Franciscan wonder-worker and Evangelical Doctor.

sections.b: Portuguese Franciscan wonder-worker and Evangelical Doctor.

entries.d: Princess who gave everything to the poor and died at twenty-four.

sections.b: Princess who gave everything to the poor and died at twenty-four.

entries.d: First follower of Francis and foundress of the Poor Clares.

sections.b: First follower of Francis and foundress of the Poor Clares.

entries.d: Crusader king of France, the medieval model of a Christian ruler.

sections.b: Crusader king of France, the medieval model of a Christian ruler.

entries.d: The Angelic Doctor, whose Summa Theologiae remains the Church's greatest theological synthesis.

sections.b: The Angelic Doctor, whose Summa Theologiae remains the Church's greatest theological synthesis.

entries.d: The Seraphic Doctor, master of Franciscan theology and mystical wisdom.

sections.b: The Seraphic Doctor, master of Franciscan theology and mystical wisdom.

entries.d: The Universal Doctor — natural scientist, philosopher, and teacher of Aquinas.

sections.b: The Universal Doctor — natural scientist, philosopher, and teacher of Aquinas.

entries.d: Mystic, foundress, and co-patroness of Europe.

sections.b: Mystic, foundress, and co-patroness of Europe.

entries.d: Dominican mystic who persuaded the pope to return from Avignon; Doctor of the Church.

sections.b: Dominican mystic who persuaded the pope to return from Avignon; Doctor of the Church.

entries.d: The Maid of Orléans, burned at the stake at nineteen and canonized in 1920.

sections.b: The Maid of Orléans, burned at the stake at nineteen and canonized in 1920.

entries.d: Chancellor of England, martyred for refusing the royal supremacy: 'the King's good servant, but God's first.'

sections.b: Chancellor of England, martyred for refusing the royal supremacy: 'the King's good servant, but God's first.'

entries.d: The only English bishop to resist Henry VIII; created cardinal in prison, then beheaded.

sections.b: The only English bishop to resist Henry VIII; created cardinal in prison, then beheaded.

entries.d: Foundress of the Ursulines, the Church's first teaching order of women.

sections.b: Foundress of the Ursulines, the Church's first teaching order of women.

entries.d: The humble visionary to whom Our Lady of Guadalupe appeared.

sections.b: The humble visionary to whom Our Lady of Guadalupe appeared.

entries.d: Jesuit missionary to India and Japan, who died within sight of China; patron of missions.
